subject: ap-precalculus
title: Exponential Decay Modeling: Radioactive Isotope & Semi-Log Analysis
stimulus:
A laboratory sample of a radioactive isotope initially contains $500$ milligrams. Scientists measure the remaining amount at various times (in hours) and record the following data:

| Time $t$ (hours) | Amount $A$ (mg) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 500 |
| 10 | 341 |
| 25 | 191 |
| 40 | 107 |
| 60 | 43 |

A scientist proposes the model $A(t) = a \cdot b^t$ to represent the amount remaining, in milligrams, at time $t$ hours.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 60$ to write two equations that can be used to find the values for constants $a$ and $b$ in the expression for $A(t)$.

(ii) Find the values for $a$ and $b$ as decimal approximations. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Decimal approximations must be correct to three decimal places. The first equation immediately gives $a = 500$. Full credit for $b$ if supporting work shows $b = \left(\frac{43}{500}\right)^{1/60}$ or equivalent logarithmic approach. Accept $b \approx 0.963$ and $A(t) = 500 \cdot (0.963)^t$ as a complete response for both points.

scoring_points.description: Two correct equations: $a \cdot b^0 = 500$ (giving $a = 500$) and $a \cdot b^{60} = 43$; equivalently $500 \cdot b^{60} = 43$

required_elements: Two correct equations: $a \cdot b^0 = 500$ (giving $a = 500$) and $a \cdot b^{60} = 43$; equivalently $500 \cdot b^{60} = 43$
alternatives.correct_example: Two correct equations: $a \cdot b^0 = 500$ (giving $a = 500$) and $a \cdot b^{60} = 43$; equivalently $500 \cdot b^{60} = 43$

parts.prompt:
(i) Find the average rate of change of $A$, in milligrams per hour, from $t = 10$ to $t = 40$. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Explain what the average rate of change found in part (i) means in the context of this problem.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), the answer is exactly $-7.8$ mg/hr; accept any value from $-7.85$ to $-7.75$ if using rounded data. Units are not required in part (i) but are expected in part (ii). In part (ii), all three components (direction, rate, time interval) must be present. A response that omits the time interval or omits the decreasing nature does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Correct average rate of change $\frac{A(40) - A(10)}{40 - 10} = \frac{107 - 341}{30} = \frac{-234}{30} = -7.8$ milligrams per hour (exact); supporting work must show a quotient using the data values

required_elements: Correct average rate of change $\frac{A(40) - A(10)}{40 - 10} = \frac{107 - 341}{30} = \frac{-234}{30} = -7.8$ milligrams per hour (exact); supporting work must show a quotient using the data values
alternatives.correct_example: Correct average rate of change $\frac{A(40) - A(10)}{40 - 10} = \frac{107 - 341}{30} = \frac{-234}{30} = -7.8$ milligrams per hour (exact); supporting work must show a quotient using the data values

scoring_points.description: Contextual interpretation must state (a) the amount of isotope is decreasing, (b) the rate is approximately $7.8$ milligrams per hour (or the value found in (i)), and (c) the time interval from $t = 10$ to $t = 40$ hours — e.g., 'On average, the radioactive sample lost $7.8$ milligrams per hour between $10$ and $40$ hours after the start'

required_elements: Contextual interpretation must state (a) the amount of isotope is decreasing, (b) the rate is approximately $7.8$ milligrams per hour (or the value found in (i)), and (c) the time interval from $t = 10$ to $t = 40$ hours — e.g., 'On average, the radioactive sample lost $7.8$ milligrams per hour between $10$ and $40$ hours after the start'
alternatives.correct_example: Contextual interpretation must state (a) the amount of isotope is decreasing, (b) the rate is approximately $7.8$ milligrams per hour (or the value found in (i)), and (c) the time interval from $t = 10$ to $t = 40$ hours — e.g., 'On average, the radioactive sample lost $7.8$ milligrams per hour between $10$ and $40$ hours after the start'

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 60$ to find the slope of the line on a semi-log plot of $\ln(A)$ versus $t$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) A different isotope is plotted on a similar semi-log plot of $\ln(A)$ versus $t$, and the data appear approximately linear with a steeper negative slope. What does a steeper negative slope indicate about that isotope compared to the original?
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), exact value is $\frac{\ln(43/500)}{60} = \frac{\ln(0.086)}{60} \approx -0.0409$. Note that $\ln(b) \approx \ln(0.963) \approx -0.0377$ (from part a values); the slope from data endpoints $(-0.0409)$ may differ slightly from $\ln(b)$ due to rounding in the data. Accept either calculation method. For part (ii), a response earns the point by connecting a more negative slope to faster decay or shorter half-life. A response that only says 'decays faster' without connecting to the semi-log slope context earns the point if it is otherwise clear.

scoring_points.description: Correct slope calculation — slope $= \frac{\ln(43) - \ln(500)}{60 - 0} = \frac{3.761 - 6.215}{60} = \frac{-2.454}{60} \approx -0.0409$ (accept values in $[-0.042, -0.040]$); supporting work must show the quotient using $\ln$ of the data values

required_elements: Correct slope calculation — slope $= \frac{\ln(43) - \ln(500)}{60 - 0} = \frac{3.761 - 6.215}{60} = \frac{-2.454}{60} \approx -0.0409$ (accept values in $[-0.042, -0.040]$); supporting work must show the quotient using $\ln$ of the data values
alternatives.correct_example: Correct slope calculation — slope $= \frac{\ln(43) - \ln(500)}{60 - 0} = \frac{3.761 - 6.215}{60} = \frac{-2.454}{60} \approx -0.0409$ (accept values in $[-0.042, -0.040]$); supporting work must show the quotient using $\ln$ of the data values

scoring_points.description: Correct interpretation — a steeper negative slope on the semi-log plot indicates a faster rate of decay; the isotope loses a larger fraction of its mass per hour, so it has a shorter half-life than the original isotope

required_elements: Correct interpretation — a steeper negative slope on the semi-log plot indicates a faster rate of decay; the isotope loses a larger fraction of its mass per hour, so it has a shorter half-life than the original isotope
alternatives.correct_example: Correct interpretation — a steeper negative slope on the semi-log plot indicates a faster rate of decay; the isotope loses a larger fraction of its mass per hour, so it has a shorter half-life than the original isotope
